question:
Let $a_1, a_2, \dots, a_n$ be real numbers, and let $b_1, b_2, \dots,
b_n$ be distinct positive integers. Suppose that there is a polynomial
$f(x)$ satisfying the identity
\[
(1-x)^n f(x) = 1 + \sum_{i=1}^n a_i x^{b_i}.
\]
Find a simple expression (not involving any sums) for $f(1)$ in terms
of $b_1, b_2, \dots, b_n$ and $n$ (but independent of $a_1, a_2,
\dots, a_n$).
solution:
Solution 1. For \( j \geq 1 \), let \( (b)_{j} \) denote \( b(b-1) \cdots(b-j+1) \). For \( 0 \leq j \leq n \), differentiating the identity \( j \) times and putting \( x=1 \) (or alternatively substituting \( x=y+1 \) and equating coefficients) yields
\[
\begin{aligned}
0 & =1+\sum a_{i} \\
0 & =\sum a_{i} b_{i} \\
0 & =\sum a_{i}\left(b_{i}\right)_{2} \\
& \vdots \\
0 & =\sum a_{i}\left(b_{i}\right)_{n-1} \\
(-1)^{n} n!f(1) & =\sum a_{i}\left(b_{i}\right)_{n}
\end{aligned}
\]

In other words, \( A \mathbf{v}=0 \), where
\[
A=\left(\begin{array}{ccccc}
-1 & 1 & 1 & \cdots & 1 \\
0 & b_{1} & b_{2} & \cdots & b_{n} \\
0 & \left(b_{1}\right)_{2} & \left(b_{2}\right)_{2} & \cdots & \left(b_{n}\right)_{2} \\
\vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\
0 & \left(b_{1}\right)_{n-1} & \left(b_{2}\right)_{n-1} & \cdots & \left(b_{n}\right)_{n-1} \\
(-1)^{n} n!f(1) & \left(b_{1}\right)_{n} & \left(b_{2}\right)_{n} & \cdots & \left(b_{n}\right)_{n}
\end{array}\right) \quad \text { and } \quad \mathbf{v}=\left(\begin{array}{c}
-1 \\
a_{1} \\
a_{2} \\
\vdots \\
a_{n}
\end{array}\right)
\]

Since \( \mathbf{v} \neq 0 \), \( \operatorname{det} A=0 \). Since \( (b)_{j} \) is a monic polynomial of degree \( j \) in \( b \) with no constant term, we can add a linear combination of rows \( 2,3, \ldots, k \) to row \( k+1 \), for \( 2 \leq k \leq n \), to obtain
\[
A^{\prime}=\left(\begin{array}{ccccc}
-1 & 1 & 1 & \cdots & 1 \\
0 & b_{1} & b_{2} & \cdots & b_{n} \\
0 & b_{1}^{2} & b_{2}^{2} & \cdots & b_{n}^{2} \\
\vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\
0 & b_{1}^{n-1} & b_{2}^{n-1} & \cdots & b_{n}^{n-1} \\
(-1)^{n} n!f(1) & b_{1}^{n} & b_{2}^{n} & \cdots & b_{n}^{n}
\end{array}\right)
\]

Expanding by minors along the first column yields \( 0=\operatorname{det} A^{\prime}=-\operatorname{det}\left(V^{\prime}\right)+ \) \( n!f(1) \operatorname{det}(V) \) where
\[
V=\left(\begin{array}{cccc}
1 & 1 & \cdots & 1 \\
b_{1} & b_{2} & \cdots & b_{n} \\
b_{1}^{2} & b_{2}^{2} & \cdots & b_{n}^{2} \\
\vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\
b_{1}^{n-1} & b_{2}^{n-1} & \cdots & b_{n}^{n-1}
\end{array}\right) \text { and } V^{\prime}=\left(\begin{array}{cccc}
b_{1} & b_{2} & \cdots & b_{n} \\
b_{1}^{2} & b_{2}^{2} & \cdots & b_{n}^{2} \\
\vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\
b_{1}^{n-1} & b_{2}^{n-1} & \cdots & b_{n}^{n-1} \\
b_{1}^{n} & b_{2}^{n} & \cdots & b_{n}^{n}
\end{array}\right)
\]

Factoring \( b_{i} \) out of the \( i \) th column of \( V^{\prime} \) shows that \( \operatorname{det}\left(V^{\prime}\right)=b_{1} b_{2} \cdots b_{n} \operatorname{det}(V) \). Hence \( -b_{1} b_{2} \cdots b_{n} \operatorname{det}(V)+n!f(1) \operatorname{det}(V)=0 \). Since the \( b_{i} \) are distinct, \( \operatorname{det}(V) \neq 0 \) (see below). Thus \( f(1)=b_{1} b_{2} \cdots b_{n} / n \) !.

Remark (The Vandermonde determinant). The matrix \( V \) is called the Vandermonde matrix. Its determinant \( D \) is a polynomial of total degree \( \binom{n}{2} \) in the \( b_{i} \), and \( D \) vanishes whenever \( b_{i}=b_{j} \) for \( i>j \), so \( D \) is divisible by \( b_{i}-b_{j} \) whenever \( i>j \). These \( b_{i}-b_{j} \) have no common factor, so \( \prod_{i>j}\left(b_{i}-b_{j}\right) \) divides \( D \). But this product also has total degree \( \binom{n}{2} \), and the coefficient of \( b_{2} b_{3}^{2} \cdots b_{n}^{n-1} \) in \( D \) and in \( \prod_{i>j}\left(b_{i}-b_{j}\right) \) both equal 1, so \( D=\prod_{i>j}\left(b_{i}-b_{j}\right) \). In particular, if the \( b_{i} \) are distinct numbers, then \( D \neq 0 \). See Problem 1941/14(ii) [PutnamI, p. 17] for an extension, and 1999B5 for another application.

Solution 2. Subtract 1 from both sides, set \( x=e^{t} \), and expand the left-hand side in a power series. Since \( 1-e^{t}=t+ \) (higher order terms), we get
\[
-1+(-1)^{n} f(1) t^{n}+(\text { higher order terms })=\sum_{i=1}^{n} a_{i} e^{b_{i} t}
\]

The right-hand side \( F(t) \) satisfies the linear differential equation
\[
F^{(n)}(t)-\left(b_{1}+\cdots+b_{n}\right) F^{(n-1)}(t)+\cdots+(-1)^{n} b_{1} b_{2} \cdots b_{n} F(t)=0
\]
with characteristic polynomial \( p(z)=\left(z-b_{1}\right)\left(z-b_{2}\right) \cdots\left(z-b_{n}\right) \). On the other hand, from the left-hand side of (1) we see that \( F(0)=-1, F^{(i)}=0 \) for \( i=1, \ldots, n-1 \), and \( F^{(n)}(0)=(-1)^{n} f(1) n \) !. Hence taking \( t=0 \) in (2) yields
\[
(-1)^{n} f(1) n!-0+0-0+\cdots+(-1)^{n} b_{1} b_{2} \cdots b_{n}(-1)=0
\]
so \( f(1)=b_{1} b_{2} \cdots b_{n} / n! \).

descriptive_long.question:
Let $coeffone, coefftwo, \dots, coeffenn$ be real numbers, and let $baseone, basetwo, \dots,
basenenn$ be distinct positive integers. Suppose that there is a polynomial
$polyfun(variable)$ satisfying the identity
\[
(1-variable)^{countnum} polyfun(variable) = 1 + \sum_{summindx=1}^{countnum} coeffgen \, variable^{basegen}.
\]
Find a simple expression (not involving any sums) for $polyfun(1)$ in terms
of $baseone, basetwo, \dots, basenenn$ and $countnum$ (but independent of $coeffone, coefftwo,
\dots, coeffenn$).
descriptive_long.solution:
Solution 1. For \( iterind \geq 1 \), let \( (basenum)_{iterind} \) denote \( basenum(basenum-1) \cdots(basenum-iterind+1) \). For \( 0 \leq iterind \leq countnum \), differentiating the identity \( iterind \) times and putting \( variable=1 \) (or alternatively substituting \( variable=y+1 \) and equating coefficients) yields
\[
\begin{aligned}
0 & =1+\sum coeffgen \\
0 & =\sum coeffgen basegen \\
0 & =\sum coeffgen\left(basegen\right)_{2} \\
& \vdots \\
0 & =\sum coeffgen\left(basegen\right)_{countnum-1} \\
(-1)^{countnum} countnum! \, polyfun(1) & =\sum coeffgen\left(basegen\right)_{countnum}
\end{aligned}
\]

In other words, \( bigmatrix \mathbf{vecentry}=0 \), where
\[
bigmatrix=\left(\begin{array}{ccccc}
-1 & 1 & 1 & \cdots & 1 \\
0 & baseone & basetwo & \cdots & basenenn \\
0 & \left(baseone\right)_{2} & \left(basetwo\right)_{2} & \cdots & \left(basenenn\right)_{2} \\
\vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\
0 & \left(baseone\right)_{countnum-1} & \left(basetwo\right)_{countnum-1} & \cdots & \left(basenenn\right)_{countnum-1} \\
(-1)^{countnum} countnum! \, polyfun(1) & \left(baseone\right)_{countnum} & \left(basetwo\right)_{countnum} & \cdots & \left(basenenn\right)_{countnum}
\end{array}\right) \quad \text { and } \quad \mathbf{vecentry}=\left(\begin{array}{c}
-1 \\
coeffone \\
coefftwo \\
\vdots \\
coeffenn
\end{array}\right)
\]

Since \( \mathbf{vecentry} \neq 0 \), \( \operatorname{det} bigmatrix=0 \). Since \( (basenum)_{iterind} \) is a monic polynomial of degree \( iterind \) in \( basenum \) with no constant term, we can add a linear combination of rows \( 2,3, \ldots, rowindx \) to row \( rowindx+1 \), for \( 2 \leq rowindx \leq countnum \), to obtain
\[
bigmatrix^{\prime}=\left(\begin{array}{ccccc}
-1 & 1 & 1 & \cdots & 1 \\
0 & baseone & basetwo & \cdots & basenenn \\
0 & baseone^{2} & basetwo^{2} & \cdots & basenenn^{2} \\
\vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\
0 & baseone^{countnum-1} & basetwo^{countnum-1} & \cdots & basenenn^{countnum-1} \\
(-1)^{countnum} countnum! \, polyfun(1) & baseone^{countnum} & basetwo^{countnum} & \cdots & basenenn^{countnum}
\end{array}\right)
\]

Expanding by minors along the first column yields \( 0=\operatorname{det} bigmatrix^{\prime}=-\operatorname{det}\left(vandermn^{\prime}\right)+ \)
\( countnum! \, polyfun(1) \operatorname{det}(vandermn) \) where
\[
vandermn=\left(\begin{array}{cccc}
1 & 1 & \cdots & 1 \\
baseone & basetwo & \cdots & basenenn \\
baseone^{2} & basetwo^{2} & \cdots & basenenn^{2} \\
\vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\
baseone^{countnum-1} & basetwo^{countnum-1} & \cdots & basenenn^{countnum-1}
\end{array}\right) \text { and } vandermn^{\prime}=\left(\begin{array}{cccc}
baseone & basetwo & \cdots & basenenn \\
baseone^{2} & basetwo^{2} & \cdots & basenenn^{2} \\
\vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\
baseone^{countnum-1} & basetwo^{countnum-1} & \cdots & basenenn^{countnum-1} \\
baseone^{countnum} & basetwo^{countnum} & \cdots & basenenn^{countnum}
\end{array}\right)
\]

Factoring \( basegen \) out of the \( \text{column} \) corresponding to it in \( vandermn^{\prime} \) shows that \( \operatorname{det}(vandermn^{\prime})=baseone basetwo \cdots basenenn \operatorname{det}(vandermn) \). Hence \( -baseone basetwo \cdots basenenn \operatorname{det}(vandermn)+countnum! \, polyfun(1) \operatorname{det}(vandermn)=0 \). Since the \( basegen \) are distinct, \( \operatorname{det}(vandermn) \neq 0 \) (see below). Thus \( polyfun(1)=baseone basetwo \cdots basenenn / countnum! \).

Remark (The Vandermonde determinant). The matrix \( vandermn \) is called the Vandermonde matrix. Its determinant \( vanddet \) is a polynomial of total degree \( \binom{countnum}{2} \) in the \( basegen \), and \( vanddet \) vanishes whenever two of the \( basegen \) coincide, so \( vanddet \) is divisible by the differences of any two of them. These differences have no common factor, so their product divides \( vanddet \). But this product also has total degree \( \binom{countnum}{2} \), and the leading coefficients agree, so \( vanddet \) equals that product. In particular, if the \( basegen \) are distinct numbers, then \( vanddet \neq 0 \).

Solution 2. Subtract 1 from both sides, set \( variable=e^{smallpar} \), and expand the left-hand side in a power series. Since \( 1-e^{smallpar}=smallpar+ \) (higher order terms), we get
\[
-1+(-1)^{countnum} polyfun(1) smallpar^{countnum}+(\text { higher order terms })=\sum_{summindx=1}^{countnum} coeffgen e^{basegen smallpar}
\]

The right-hand side \( seriesfx(smallpar) \) satisfies the linear differential equation
\[
seriesfx^{(countnum)}(smallpar)-\left(baseone+\cdots+basenenn\right) seriesfx^{(countnum-1)}(smallpar)+\cdots+(-1)^{countnum} baseone basetwo \cdots basenenn \, seriesfx(smallpar)=0
\]
with characteristic polynomial \( charpoly(rootvar)=\left(rootvar-baseone\right)\left(rootvar-basetwo\right) \cdots\left(rootvar-basenenn\right) \). On the other hand, from the left-hand side of (1) we see that \( seriesfx(0)=-1, seriesfx^{(summindx)}(0)=0 \) for \( summindx=1, \ldots, countnum-1 \), and \( seriesfx^{(countnum)}(0)=(-1)^{countnum} polyfun(1) countnum! \). Hence taking \( smallpar=0 \) in (2) yields
\[
(-1)^{countnum} polyfun(1) countnum!-0+0-0+\cdots+(-1)^{countnum} baseone basetwo \cdots basenenn(-1)=0
\]
so \( polyfun(1)=baseone basetwo \cdots basenenn / countnum! \).

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Let $pineapple, watermelon, \dots, raspberry$ be real numbers, and let $bluegrass, elderberry, \dots,
snowberry$ be distinct positive integers. Suppose that there is a polynomial
$harvests(longitude)$ satisfying the identity
\[
(1-longitude)^{aftershock} harvests(longitude) = 1 + \sum_{shoreline=1}^{aftershock} strawberry longitude^{corncakes}.
\]
Find a simple expression (not involving any sums) for $harvests(1)$ in terms
of $bluegrass, elderberry, \dots, snowberry$ and $aftershock$ (but independent of $pineapple, watermelon,
\dots, raspberry$).
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution 1. For \( coastline \geq 1 \), let \( (parchment)_{coastline} \) denote \( parchment(parchment-1) \cdots(parchment-coastline+1) \). For \( 0 \leq coastline \leq aftershock \), differentiating the identity \( coastline \) times and putting \( longitude=1 \) (or alternatively substituting \( longitude=y+1 \) and equating coefficients) yields
\[
\begin{aligned}
0 & =1+\sum_{shoreline} strawberry \\
0 & =\sum_{shoreline} strawberry corncakes \\
0 & =\sum_{shoreline} strawberry\left(corncakes\right)_{2} \\
& \vdots \\
0 & =\sum_{shoreline} strawberry\left(corncakes\right)_{aftershock-1} \\
(-1)^{aftershock} aftershock! harvests(1) & =\sum_{shoreline} strawberry\left(corncakes\right)_{aftershock}
\end{aligned}
\]

In other words, \( raincloud \mathbf{arrowhead}=0 \), where
\[
raincloud=\left(\begin{array}{ccccc}
-1 & 1 & 1 & \cdots & 1 \\
0 & bluegrass & elderberry & \cdots & snowberry \\
0 & \left(bluegrass\right)_{2} & \left(elderberry\right)_{2} & \cdots & \left(snowberry\right)_{2} \\
\vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\
0 & \left(bluegrass\right)_{aftershock-1} & \left(elderberry\right)_{aftershock-1} & \cdots & \left(snowberry\right)_{aftershock-1} \\
(-1)^{aftershock} aftershock! harvests(1) & \left(bluegrass\right)_{aftershock} & \left(elderberry\right)_{aftershock} & \cdots & \left(snowberry\right)_{aftershock}
\end{array}\right) \quad \text { and } \quad \mathbf{arrowhead}=\left(\begin{array}{c}
-1 \\
pineapple \\
watermelon \\
\vdots \\
raspberry
\end{array}\right)
\]

Since \( \mathbf{arrowhead} \neq 0 \), \( \operatorname{det} raincloud=0 \). Since \( (parchment)_{coastline} \) is a monic polynomial of degree \( coastline \) in \( parchment \) with no constant term, we can add a linear combination of rows \( 2,3, \ldots, sandstorm \) to row \( sandstorm+1 \), for \( 2 \leq sandstorm \leq aftershock \), to obtain
\[
raincloud^{\prime}=\left(\begin{array}{ccccc}
-1 & 1 & 1 & \cdots & 1 \\
0 & bluegrass & elderberry & \cdots & snowberry \\
0 & bluegrass^{2} & elderberry^{2} & \cdots & snowberry^{2} \\
\vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\
0 & bluegrass^{aftershock-1} & elderberry^{aftershock-1} & \cdots & snowberry^{aftershock-1} \\
(-1)^{aftershock} aftershock! harvests(1) & bluegrass^{aftershock} & elderberry^{aftershock} & \cdots & snowberry^{aftershock}
\end{array}\right)
\]

Expanding by minors along the first column yields \( 0=\operatorname{det} raincloud^{\prime}=-\operatorname{det}\left(foxgloves^{\prime}\right)+ aftershock! harvests(1) \operatorname{det}(foxgloves) \) where
\[
foxgloves=\left(\begin{array}{cccc}
1 & 1 & \cdots & 1 \\
bluegrass & elderberry & \cdots & snowberry \\
bluegrass^{2} & elderberry^{2} & \cdots & snowberry^{2} \\
\vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\
bluegrass^{aftershock-1} & elderberry^{aftershock-1} & \cdots & snowberry^{aftershock-1}
\end{array}\right) \text { and } foxgloves^{\prime}=\left(\begin{array}{cccc}
bluegrass & elderberry & \cdots & snowberry \\
bluegrass^{2} & elderberry^{2} & \cdots & snowberry^{2} \\
\vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\
bluegrass^{aftershock-1} & elderberry^{aftershock-1} & \cdots & snowberry^{aftershock-1} \\
bluegrass^{aftershock} & elderberry^{aftershock} & \cdots & snowberry^{aftershock}
\end{array}\right)
\]

Factoring \( corncakes \) out of the \( shoreline \) th column of \( foxgloves^{\prime} \) shows that \( \operatorname{det}\left(foxgloves^{\prime}\right)=bluegrass\, elderberry \cdots snowberry \operatorname{det}(foxgloves) \). Hence \( -bluegrass\, elderberry \cdots snowberry \operatorname{det}(foxgloves)+aftershock! harvests(1) \operatorname{det}(foxgloves)=0 \). Since the \( corncakes \) are distinct, \( \operatorname{det}(foxgloves) \neq 0 \) (see below). Thus \( harvests(1)=bluegrass\, elderberry \cdots snowberry / aftershock! \).

Remark (The Vandermonde determinant). The matrix \( foxgloves \) is called the Vandermonde matrix. Its determinant \( snowflake \) is a polynomial of total degree \( \binom{aftershock}{2} \) in the \( corncakes \), and \( snowflake \) vanishes whenever \( corncakes=corncakes \) for \( shoreline>coastline \), so \( snowflake \) is divisible by \( corncakes-corncakes \) whenever \( shoreline>coastline \). These \( corncakes-corncakes \) have no common factor, so \( \prod_{shoreline>coastline}\left(corncakes-corncakes\right) \) divides \( snowflake \). But this product also has total degree \( \binom{aftershock}{2} \), and the coefficient of \( elderberry\, elderberry^{2} \cdots snowberry^{aftershock-1} \) in \( snowflake \) and in \( \prod_{shoreline>coastline}\left(corncakes-corncakes\right) \) both equal 1, so \( snowflake=\prod_{shoreline>coastline}\left(corncakes-corncakes\right) \). In particular, if the \( corncakes \) are distinct numbers, then \( snowflake \neq 0 \). See Problem 1941/14(ii) [PutnamI, p. 17] for an extension, and 1999B5 for another application.

Solution 2. Subtract 1 from both sides, set \( longitude=e^{silvermaple} \), and expand the left-hand side in a power series. Since \( 1-e^{silvermaple}=silvermaple+ \) (higher order terms), we get
\[
-1+(-1)^{aftershock} harvests(1) silvermaple^{aftershock}+(\text { higher order terms })=\sum_{shoreline=1}^{aftershock} strawberry e^{corncakes silvermaple}
\]

The right-hand side \( starlight(silvermaple) \) satisfies the linear differential equation
\[
starlight^{(aftershock)}(silvermaple)-\left(bluegrass+\cdots+snowberry\right) starlight^{(aftershock-1)}(silvermaple)+\cdots+(-1)^{aftershock} bluegrass\, elderberry \cdots snowberry\, starlight(silvermaple)=0
\]
with characteristic polynomial \( lighthouse(windspeed)=\left(windspeed-bluegrass\right)\left(windspeed-elderberry\right) \cdots\left(windspeed-snowberry\right) \). On the other hand, from the left-hand side of (1) we see that \( starlight(0)=-1, starlight^{(shoreline)}=0 \) for \( shoreline=1, \ldots, aftershock-1 \), and \( starlight^{(aftershock)}(0)=(-1)^{aftershock} harvests(1) aftershock! \). Hence taking \( silvermaple=0 \) in (2) yields
\[
(-1)^{aftershock} harvests(1) aftershock!-0+0-0+\cdots+(-1)^{aftershock} bluegrass\, elderberry \cdots snowberry(-1)=0
\]
so \( harvests(1)=bluegrass\, elderberry \cdots snowberry / aftershock! \).

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Let $firstconstant, secondconstant, \dots, lastconstant$ be real numbers, and let $firststatic, secondstatic, \dots, laststatic$ be distinct positive integers. Suppose that there is a polynomial $nonpolyfunc(constantval)$ satisfying the identity
\[
(1-constantval)^{infinitecount} nonpolyfunc(constantval) = 1 + \sum_{fixposval=1}^{infinitecount} genericconstant\, constantval^{genericstatic}.
\]
Find a simple expression (not involving any sums) for $nonpolyfunc(1)$ in terms of $firststatic, secondstatic, \dots, laststatic$ and $infinitecount$ (but independent of $firstconstant, secondconstant, \dots, lastconstant$).
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution 1. For \( maximus \ge 1 \), let \( (staticvar)_{maximus} \) denote \( staticvar(staticvar-1)\cdots(staticvar-maximus+1) \). For \( 0 \le maximus \le infinitecount \), differentiating the identity \( maximus \) times and putting \( constantval=1 \) (or alternatively substituting \( constantval=y+1 \) and equating coefficients) yields
\[
\begin{aligned}
0 & =1+\sum genericconstant \\
0 & =\sum genericconstant genericstatic \\
0 & =\sum genericconstant\left(genericstatic\right)_{2} \\
& \vdots \\
0 & =\sum genericconstant\left(genericstatic\right)_{infinitecount-1} \\
(-1)^{infinitecount} infinitecount! nonpolyfunc(1) & =\sum genericconstant\left(genericstatic\right)_{infinitecount}
\end{aligned}
\]

In other words, \( scalarset \mathbf{scalarval}=0 \), where
\[
scalarset=\left(\begin{array}{ccccc}
-1 & 1 & 1 & \cdots & 1 \\
0 & firststatic & secondstatic & \cdots & laststatic \\
0 & \left(firststatic\right)_{2} & \left(secondstatic\right)_{2} & \cdots & \left(laststatic\right)_{2} \\
\vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\
0 & \left(firststatic\right)_{infinitecount-1} & \left(secondstatic\right)_{infinitecount-1} & \cdots & \left(laststatic\right)_{infinitecount-1} \\
(-1)^{infinitecount} infinitecount! nonpolyfunc(1) & \left(firststatic\right)_{infinitecount} & \left(secondstatic\right)_{infinitecount} & \cdots & \left(laststatic\right)_{infinitecount}
\end{array}\right) \quad \text { and } \quad \mathbf{scalarval}=\left(\begin{array}{c}
-1 \\
firstconstant \\
secondconstant \\
\vdots \\
lastconstant
\end{array}\right)
\]

Since \( \mathbf{scalarval} \neq 0 \), \( \operatorname{det} scalarset=0 \). Since \( (staticvar)_{maximus} \) is a monic polynomial of degree \( maximus \) in \( staticvar \) with no constant term, we can add a linear combination of rows \( 2,3, \ldots, minimizer \) to row \( minimizer+1 \), for \( 2 \le minimizer \le infinitecount \), to obtain
\[
scalarset^{\prime}=\left(\begin{array}{ccccc}
-1 & 1 & 1 & \cdots & 1 \\
0 & firststatic & secondstatic & \cdots & laststatic \\
0 & firststatic^{2} & secondstatic^{2} & \cdots & laststatic^{2} \\
\vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\
0 & firststatic^{infinitecount-1} & secondstatic^{infinitecount-1} & \cdots & laststatic^{infinitecount-1} \\
(-1)^{infinitecount} infinitecount! nonpolyfunc(1) & firststatic^{infinitecount} & secondstatic^{infinitecount} & \cdots & laststatic^{infinitecount}
\end{array}\right)
\]

Expanding by minors along the first column yields \( 0=\operatorname{det} scalarset^{\prime}=-\operatorname{det}\left(nonvanderm^{\prime}\right)+ infinitecount! nonpolyfunc(1) \operatorname{det}(nonvanderm) \) where
\[
nonvanderm=\left(\begin{array}{cccc}
1 & 1 & \cdots & 1 \\
firststatic & secondstatic & \cdots & laststatic \\
firststatic^{2} & secondstatic^{2} & \cdots & laststatic^{2} \\
\vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\
firststatic^{infinitecount-1} & secondstatic^{infinitecount-1} & \cdots & laststatic^{infinitecount-1}
\end{array}\right) \text { and } nonvanderm^{\prime}=\left(\begin{array}{cccc}
firststatic & secondstatic & \cdots & laststatic \\
firststatic^{2} & secondstatic^{2} & \cdots & laststatic^{2} \\
\vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\
firststatic^{infinitecount-1} & secondstatic^{infinitecount-1} & \cdots & laststatic^{infinitecount-1} \\
firststatic^{infinitecount} & secondstatic^{infinitecount} & \cdots & laststatic^{infinitecount}
\end{array}\right)
\]

Factoring \( firststatic, secondstatic, \ldots , laststatic \) out of the respective columns of \( nonvanderm^{\prime} \) shows that \( \operatorname{det}\left(nonvanderm^{\prime}\right)=firststatic secondstatic \cdots laststatic \operatorname{det}(nonvanderm) \). Hence \( -firststatic secondstatic \cdots laststatic \operatorname{det}(nonvanderm)+infinitecount! nonpolyfunc(1) \operatorname{det}(nonvanderm)=0 \). Since the \( firststatic, secondstatic, \ldots , laststatic \) are distinct, \( \operatorname{det}(nonvanderm) \neq 0 \) (see below). Thus \( nonpolyfunc(1)=firststatic secondstatic \cdots laststatic / infinitecount! \).

Remark (The Vandermonde determinant). The matrix \( nonvanderm \) is called the Vandermonde matrix. Its determinant \( nondetermin \) is a polynomial of total degree \( \binom{infinitecount}{2} \) in the \( firststatic, secondstatic, \ldots , laststatic \), and \( nondetermin \) vanishes whenever \( firststatic=secondstatic \) for \( fixposval>maximus \), so \( nondetermin \) is divisible by \( firststatic-secondstatic \) whenever \( fixposval>maximus \). These \( firststatic-secondstatic \) have no common factor, so \( \prod_{fixposval>maximus}\left(firststatic-secondstatic\right) \) divides \( nondetermin \). But this product also has total degree \( \binom{infinitecount}{2} \), and the coefficient of \( secondstatic firststatic^{2} \cdots laststatic^{infinitecount-1} \) in \( nondetermin \) and in \( \prod_{fixposval>maximus}\left(firststatic-secondstatic\right) \) both equal 1, so \( nondetermin=\prod_{fixposval>maximus}\left(firststatic-secondstatic\right) \). In particular, if the \( firststatic, secondstatic, \ldots , laststatic \) are distinct numbers, then \( nondetermin \neq 0 \). See Problem 1941/14(ii) [PutnamI, p. 17] for an extension, and 1999B5 for another application.

Solution 2. Subtract 1 from both sides, set \( constantval=e^{spacedim} \), and expand the left-hand side in a power series. Since \( 1-e^{spacedim}=spacedim+ \) (higher order terms), we get
\[
-1+(-1)^{infinitecount} nonpolyfunc(1) spacedim^{infinitecount}+(\text { higher order terms })=\sum_{fixposval=1}^{infinitecount} genericconstant e^{genericstatic spacedim}
\]

The right-hand side \( staticvalue(spacedim) \) satisfies the linear differential equation
\[
staticvalue^{(infinitecount)}(spacedim)-\left(firststatic+\cdots+laststatic\right) staticvalue^{(infinitecount-1)}(spacedim)+\cdots+(-1)^{infinitecount} firststatic secondstatic \cdots laststatic\, staticvalue(spacedim)=0
\]
with characteristic polynomial \( nonpolyexp(vertexval)=\left(vertexval-firststatic\right)\left(vertexval-secondstatic\right) \cdots\left(vertexval-laststatic\right) \). On the other hand, from the left-hand side of (1) we see that \( staticvalue(0)=-1, staticvalue^{(maximus)}=0 \) for \( maximus=1, \ldots, infinitecount-1 \), and \( staticvalue^{(infinitecount)}(0)=(-1)^{infinitecount} nonpolyfunc(1) infinitecount! \). Hence taking \( spacedim=0 \) in (2) yields
\[
(-1)^{infinitecount} nonpolyfunc(1) infinitecount!-0+0-0+\cdots+(-1)^{infinitecount} firststatic secondstatic \cdots laststatic(-1)=0
\]
so \( nonpolyfunc(1)=firststatic secondstatic \cdots laststatic / infinitecount! \).

garbled_string.question:
Let $qzxwvtnp, hjgrksla, \dots, mflqpsow$ be real numbers, and let $zictemah, rvoupnla, \dots,
sweldeax$ be distinct positive integers. Suppose that there is a polynomial
$vgczyplk(sbnifero)$ satisfying the identity
\[
(1-sbnifero)^{kodyunwe} vgczyplk(sbnifero) = 1 + \sum_{glsafezn=1}^{kodyunwe} rnbdakue \, sbnifero^{ljotgcra}.
\]
Find a simple expression (not involving any sums) for $vgczyplk(1)$ in terms
of $zictemah, rvoupnla, \dots, sweldeax$ and $kodyunwe$ (but independent of $qzxwvtnp, hjgrksla,
\dots, mflqpsow$).
garbled_string.solution:
Solution 1. For \( uvlaznqo \geq 1 \), let \( (vifranco)_{uvlaznqo} \) denote \( vifranco(vifranco-1) \cdots(vifranco-uvlaznqo+1) \). For \( 0 \leq uvlaznqo \leq kodyunwe \), differentiating the identity \( uvlaznqo \) times and putting \( sbnifero=1 \) (or alternatively substituting \( sbnifero=y+1 \) and equating coefficients) yields
\[
\begin{aligned}
0 & =1+\sum rnbdakue \\
0 & =\sum rnbdakue \, ljotgcra \\
0 & =\sum rnbdakue\left(ljotgcra\right)_{2} \\
& \vdots \\
0 & =\sum rnbdakue\left(ljotgcra\right)_{kodyunwe-1} \\
(-1)^{kodyunwe} kodyunwe! \, vgczyplk(1) & =\sum rnbdakue\left(ljotgcra\right)_{kodyunwe}
\end{aligned}
\]

In other words, \( soactrmv \mathbf{ylwpxdqe}=0 \), where
\[
soactrmv=\left(\begin{array}{ccccc}
-1 & 1 & 1 & \cdots & 1 \\
0 & zictemah & rvoupnla & \cdots & sweldeax \\
0 & \left(zictemah\right)_{2} & \left(rvoupnla\right)_{2} & \cdots & \left(sweldeax\right)_{2} \\
\vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\
0 & \left(zictemah\right)_{kodyunwe-1} & \left(rvoupnla\right)_{kodyunwe-1} & \cdots & \left(sweldeax\right)_{kodyunwe-1} \\
(-1)^{kodyunwe} kodyunwe! \, vgczyplk(1) & \left(zictemah\right)_{kodyunwe} & \left(rvoupnla\right)_{kodyunwe} & \cdots & \left(sweldeax\right)_{kodyunwe}
\end{array}\right) \quad \text { and } \quad \mathbf{ylwpxdqe}=\left(\begin{array}{c}
-1 \\
qzxwvtnp \\
hjgrksla \\
\vdots \\
mflqpsow
\end{array}\right)
\]

Since \( \mathbf{ylwpxdqe} \neq 0 \), \( \operatorname{det} soactrmv=0 \). Since \( (vifranco)_{uvlaznqo} \) is a monic polynomial of degree \( uvlaznqo \) in \( vifranco \) with no constant term, we can add a linear combination of rows \( 2,3, \ldots, dpseirgw \) to row \( dpseirgw+1 \), for \( 2 \leq dpseirgw \leq kodyunwe \), to obtain
\[
soactrmv^{\prime}=\left(\begin{array}{ccccc}
-1 & 1 & 1 & \cdots & 1 \\
0 & zictemah & rvoupnla & \cdots & sweldeax \\
0 & zictemah^{2} & rvoupnla^{2} & \cdots & sweldeax^{2} \\
\vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\
0 & zictemah^{kodyunwe-1} & rvoupnla^{kodyunwe-1} & \cdots & sweldeax^{kodyunwe-1} \\
(-1)^{kodyunwe} kodyunwe! \, vgczyplk(1) & zictemah^{kodyunwe} & rvoupnla^{kodyunwe} & \cdots & sweldeax^{kodyunwe}
\end{array}\right)
\]

Expanding by minors along the first column yields
\( 0=\operatorname{det} soactrmv^{\prime}=-\operatorname{det}\left(zmhuqtga^{\prime}\right)+kodyunwe! \, vgczyplk(1)\operatorname{det}(zmhuqtga) \)
where
\[
zmhuqtga=\left(\begin{array}{cccc}
1 & 1 & \cdots & 1 \\
zictemah & rvoupnla & \cdots & sweldeax \\
zictemah^{2} & rvoupnla^{2} & \cdots & sweldeax^{2} \\
\vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\
zictemah^{kodyunwe-1} & rvoupnla^{kodyunwe-1} & \cdots & sweldeax^{kodyunwe-1}
\end{array}\right) \quad \text { and } \quad
zmhuqtga^{\prime}=\left(\begin{array}{cccc}
zictemah & rvoupnla & \cdots & sweldeax \\
zictemah^{2} & rvoupnla^{2} & \cdots & sweldeax^{2} \\
\vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\
zictemah^{kodyunwe-1} & rvoupnla^{kodyunwe-1} & \cdots & sweldeax^{kodyunwe-1} \\
zictemah^{kodyunwe} & rvoupnla^{kodyunwe} & \cdots & sweldeax^{kodyunwe}
\end{array}\right)
\]

Factoring \( ljotgcra \) out of the \( glsafezn \)th column of \( zmhuqtga^{\prime} \) shows that \( \operatorname{det}\left(zmhuqtga^{\prime}\right)=zictemah \, rvoupnla \cdots sweldeax \, \operatorname{det}(zmhuqtga) \). Hence
\( -zictemah \, rvoupnla \cdots sweldeax \, \operatorname{det}(zmhuqtga)+kodyunwe! \, vgczyplk(1)\operatorname{det}(zmhuqtga)=0 \).
Since the \( ljotgcra \) are distinct, \( \operatorname{det}(zmhuqtga) \neq 0 \) (see below). Thus
\( vgczyplk(1)=\dfrac{zictemah \, rvoupnla \cdots sweldeax}{kodyunwe!}. \)

Remark (The Vandermonde determinant). The matrix \( zmhuqtga \) is called the Vandermonde matrix. Its determinant \( ecrvjoxb \) is a polynomial of total degree \( \binom{kodyunwe}{2} \) in the \( ljotgcra \), and \( ecrvjoxb \) vanishes whenever \( ljotgcra=ljotgcra^{\prime} \) for \( glsafezn>uvlaznqo \), so \( ecrvjoxb \) is divisible by \( ljotgcra-ljotgcra^{\prime} \) whenever \( glsafezn>uvlaznqo \). These \( ljotgcra-ljotgcra^{\prime} \) have no common factor, so \( \prod_{glsafezn>uvlaznqo}\left(ljotgcra-ljotgcra^{\prime}\right) \) divides \( ecrvjoxb \). But this product also has total degree \( \binom{kodyunwe}{2} \), and the coefficient of \( rvoupnla \, sweldeax^{kodyunwe-1} \) in \( ecrvjoxb \) and in \( \prod_{glsafezn>uvlaznqo}\left(ljotgcra-ljotgcra^{\prime}\right) \) both equal 1, so \( ecrvjoxb=\prod_{glsafezn>uvlaznqo}\left(ljotgcra-ljotgcra^{\prime}\right) \). In particular, if the \( ljotgcra \) are distinct numbers, then \( ecrvjoxb \neq 0 \). See Problem 1941/14(ii) [PutnamI, p. 17] for an extension, and 1999B5 for another application.

Solution 2. Subtract 1 from both sides, set \( sbnifero=e^{nkivchya} \), and expand the left-hand side in a power series. Since \( 1-e^{nkivchya}=nkivchya+ \) (higher order terms), we get
\[
-1+(-1)^{kodyunwe} vgczyplk(1) \, nkivchya^{kodyunwe}+(\text { higher order terms })=\sum_{glsafezn=1}^{kodyunwe} rnbdakue \, e^{ljotgcra \, nkivchya}
\]

The right-hand side \( xludsepq(nkivchya) \) satisfies the linear differential equation
\[
xludsepq^{(kodyunwe)}(nkivchya)-\left(zictemah+rvoupnla+\cdots+sweldeax\right) xludsepq^{(kodyunwe-1)}(nkivchya)+\cdots+(-1)^{kodyunwe} zictemah \, rvoupnla \cdots sweldeax \, xludsepq(nkivchya)=0
\]
with characteristic polynomial \( wdrtqmlu(bksyejph)=\left(bksyejph-zictemah\right)\left(bksyejph-rvoupnla\right) \cdots\left(bksyejph-sweldeax\right) \). On the other hand, from the left-hand side of (1) we see that \( xludsepq(0)=-1, xludsepq^{(glsafezn)}=0 \) for \( glsafezn=1, \ldots, kodyunwe-1 \), and \( xludsepq^{(kodyunwe)}(0)=(-1)^{kodyunwe} vgczyplk(1) kodyunwe! \). Hence taking \( nkivchya=0 \) in (2) yields
\[
(-1)^{kodyunwe} vgczyplk(1) kodyunwe!-0+0-0+\cdots+(-1)^{kodyunwe} zictemah \, rvoupnla \cdots sweldeax(-1)=0,
\]
so \( vgczyplk(1)=\dfrac{zictemah \, rvoupnla \cdots sweldeax}{kodyunwe!}. \)

kernel_variant.question:
Let $r,n$ be positive integers with $n\ge 2$.  
For each $k\in\{1,2,\dots ,r\}$ choose  
\[
b_{k,1},\,b_{k,2},\dots ,b_{k,n}\qquad(\text{pairwise distinct positive integers}).
\]

Assume that there exists a real polynomial in $r$ variables  
\[
f(x_{1},x_{2},\dots ,x_{r})\in\mathbb{R}[x_{1},x_{2},\dots ,x_{r}]
\]
satisfying the identity  
\[
\Bigl(\,\prod_{k=1}^{r}(1-x_{k})^{\,n}\Bigr)\,f(x_{1},\dots ,x_{r})
\;=\;
1+\sum_{i_{1}=1}^{n}\cdots\sum_{i_{r}=1}^{n}
a_{i_{1},\dots ,i_{r}}\;
x_{1}^{\,b_{1,i_{1}}}\,x_{2}^{\,b_{2,i_{2}}}\,\cdots\,x_{r}^{\,b_{r,i_{r}}}.
\tag{$\star$}
\]

(The real numbers $a_{i_{1},\dots ,i_{r}}$ are arbitrary; the hypothesis
merely guarantees that some polynomial $f$ exists for the given data.)

Find a closed-form expression, containing \emph{no} summations and independent
of the $a$'s, for  
\[
f(1,1,\dots ,1)
\]
in terms of $n$ and the numbers $b_{k,j}$.
kernel_variant.solution:
Boldface letters denote multi-indices;  
$\mathbf{0}=(0,\dots ,0)$ and $\mathbf{1}=(1,\dots ,1)$ are the constant
$r$-tuples.



1.  Factorial-Vandermonde matrices.  
For $m\ge 0$ and $t\in\mathbb{R}$ put
\[
(t)_{m}=t(t-1)\cdots(t-m+1)\qquad\bigl((t)_{0}=1\bigr).
\]
For every $k$ define the $n\times n$ matrix
\[
B^{(k)}
=\bigl[(b_{k,j})_{s}\bigr]_{0\le s\le n-1,\;1\le j\le n},
\]
a factorial Vandermonde; hence $\det B^{(k)}\neq 0$.

Set  
\[
u^{(k)}=\begin{pmatrix}-1\\0\\\vdots\\0\end{pmatrix},
\qquad
w^{(k)}=\begin{pmatrix}(b_{k,1})_{n}\\ \vdots\\ (b_{k,n})_{n}\end{pmatrix},
\]
and form the Kronecker products  
\[
B:=B^{(r)}\otimes\cdots\otimes B^{(1)},\qquad
u:=u^{(r)}\otimes\cdots\otimes u^{(1)}.
\]
All three objects have length $n^{\,r}$; $B$ is invertible.



2.  A linear system for the coefficients $a_{\,\dots}$.  
Apply $\partial^{\boldsymbol{\alpha}}$ to $(\star)$ and set
$x=\mathbf{1}$.

*  If $0\le\alpha_{k}\le n-1$ for every $k$ and
$\boldsymbol{\alpha}\neq\mathbf{0}$, the factor
$(1-x_{j})$ survives for some $j$, so the left-hand side vanishes at
$x=\mathbf{1}$.  Thus
\[
\sum_{i_{1},\dots ,i_{r}}
a_{i_{1},\dots ,i_{r}}
\prod_{k=1}^{r}(b_{k,i_{k}})_{\alpha_{k}}
=0.
\tag{1}
\]

*  For $\boldsymbol{\alpha}=\mathbf{0}$ we obtain
\[
1+\sum_{i_{1},\dots ,i_{r}}a_{i_{1},\dots ,i_{r}}=0,
\qquad\Longrightarrow\qquad
\sum_{i_{1},\dots ,i_{r}}a_{i_{1},\dots ,i_{r}}=-1.
\tag{2}
\]

Arrange the $n^{\,r}$ unknowns
$A=\operatorname{vec}[a_{i_{1},\dots ,i_{r}}]$.  
Conditions (1)-(2) are equivalent to the linear system
\[
B\,A = e,
\tag{3}
\]
where the right-hand side vector $e\in\mathbb{R}^{n^{\,r}}$ is defined by  

- \emph{Row-wise description}: $e$ has entry $-1$ in the row indexed by
$\boldsymbol{\alpha}=\mathbf{0}$ and $0$ in every other row.  

- \emph{Kronecker description}:  
\[
e=(-1)^{\,1-r}\,u,
\]
so that the very first component of $e$ equals $-1$ (not $(-1)^{\,r}$).

Because $B$ is nonsingular, (3) has the unique solution
\[
A=B^{-1}e.
\tag{4}
\]



3.  A high derivative links $f(\mathbf{1})$ to the $a$'s.  
Let $\boldsymbol{\beta}=(n,n,\dots ,n)$.  Differentiating $(\star)$ by
$\partial^{\boldsymbol{\beta}}$ and then putting $x=\mathbf{1}$ gives
\[
(-1)^{\,nr}\,(n!)^{\,r}\,f(\mathbf{1})
=\sum_{i_{1},\dots ,i_{r}}
a_{i_{1},\dots ,i_{r}}
\prod_{k=1}^{r}(b_{k,i_{k}})_{n}
=w^{\mathsf T}B^{-1}e,
\tag{5}
\]
where $w:=w^{(r)}\otimes\cdots\otimes w^{(1)}$.



4.  Kronecker factorisation.  
The scalar $w^{\mathsf T}B^{-1}e$ separates:
\[
w^{\mathsf T}B^{-1}e
=(-1)^{\,1-r}\,
\prod_{k=1}^{r}\bigl[(w^{(k)})^{\mathsf T}(B^{(k)})^{-1}u^{(k)}\bigr].
\tag{6}
\]
Thus we need the one-variable constant
\[
S_{k}:=(w^{(k)})^{\mathsf T}(B^{(k)})^{-1}u^{(k)}.
\]



5.  Evaluation of $S_{k}$.  
Fix $k$ and abbreviate $B=B^{(k)},\,w=w^{(k)},\,u=u^{(k)}$.
Cramer's rule gives
\[
B^{-1}u
=-\,\frac{\operatorname{adj}B_{\,\cdot ,1}}{\det B},
\qquad\Longrightarrow\qquad
S_{k}
=-\,\frac{w^{\mathsf T}\!\operatorname{adj}B_{\,\cdot ,1}}{\det B}.
\]
The numerator is
$\det B[1\leftarrow w]$, the determinant of the matrix obtained by
replacing the first \emph{row} of $B$ by $w^{\mathsf T}$.  A standard
factorial-Vandermonde computation yields
\[
\det B[1\leftarrow w]
=(-1)^{\,n+1}\Bigl(\prod_{j=1}^{n}b_{k,j}\Bigr)\det B,
\]
hence
\[
S_{k}=(-1)^{\,n}\prod_{j=1}^{n}b_{k,j}.
\tag{7}
\]



6.  Completion.  
Insert (7) into (6):
\[
w^{\mathsf T}B^{-1}e
=(-1)^{\,1-r}\,
\prod_{k=1}^{r}(-1)^{\,n}\prod_{j=1}^{n}b_{k,j}
=(-1)^{\,nr+1-r}\,
\prod_{k=1}^{r}\prod_{j=1}^{n}b_{k,j}.
\tag{8}
\]

Combine (5) with (8).  The factor $(-1)^{\,nr}$ cancels, leaving
\[
f(1,1,\dots ,1)
\;=\;
(-1)^{\,r-1}\;
\frac{\displaystyle\prod_{k=1}^{r}\prod_{j=1}^{n}b_{k,j}}
      {(n!)^{\,r}}.
\qquad\qquad\blacksquare
\]

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1. Higher dimensionality.  
   • The problem moves from one variable to r variables (r≥2), so the
     unknown polynomial lives in a space of significantly higher
     dimension.  
   • The number of unknown coefficients rises from n to n^{r}.  
   • Mixed partial derivatives and Kronecker-product linear algebra are
     required.

2. Additional constraints.  
   • All mixed derivatives of order < n in every direction must vanish at
     (1,…,1), producing a system of n^{r} simultaneous equations rather
     than n.

3. More sophisticated structure.  
   • The solution uses factorial Vandermonde matrices in each variable
     and their Kronecker product; this demands familiarity with tensor
     products, multi-index calculus, and properties of block determinants.

4. Deeper theory and multiple interacting concepts.  
   • One must combine multivariate calculus, combinatorial identities for
     falling factorials, determinant factorisation, and Kronecker
     geometry to isolate the single surviving mixed derivative and relate
     it to det M.  
   • The determinant evaluation generalises the classical Vandermonde
     argument to multi-linear algebra, a non-trivial extension.

Hence the enhanced variant is substantially more intricate than the
original (one variable, n equations, ordinary Vandermonde) and the
previous kernel variant (constant term 2, primes only).  Solving it
demands advanced multivariate techniques beyond routine pattern
matching.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Let $r,n$ be positive integers with $n\ge 2$.  
For each $k\in\{1,2,\dots ,r\}$ choose  
\[
b_{k,1},\,b_{k,2},\dots ,b_{k,n}\qquad(\text{pairwise distinct positive integers}).
\]

Assume that there exists a real polynomial in $r$ variables  
\[
f(x_{1},x_{2},\dots ,x_{r})\in\mathbb{R}[x_{1},x_{2},\dots ,x_{r}]
\]
satisfying the identity  
\[
\Bigl(\,\prod_{k=1}^{r}(1-x_{k})^{\,n}\Bigr)\,f(x_{1},\dots ,x_{r})
\;=\;
1+\sum_{i_{1}=1}^{n}\cdots\sum_{i_{r}=1}^{n}
a_{i_{1},\dots ,i_{r}}\;
x_{1}^{\,b_{1,i_{1}}}\,x_{2}^{\,b_{2,i_{2}}}\,\cdots\,x_{r}^{\,b_{r,i_{r}}}.
\tag{$\star$}
\]

(The real numbers $a_{i_{1},\dots ,i_{r}}$ are arbitrary; the hypothesis
merely guarantees that some polynomial $f$ exists for the given data.)

Find a closed-form expression, containing \emph{no} summations and independent
of the $a$'s, for  
\[
f(1,1,\dots ,1)
\]
in terms of $n$ and the numbers $b_{k,j}$.
original_kernel_variant.solution:
Boldface letters denote multi-indices;  
$\mathbf{0}=(0,\dots ,0)$ and $\mathbf{1}=(1,\dots ,1)$ are the constant
$r$-tuples.



1.  Factorial-Vandermonde matrices.  
For $m\ge 0$ and $t\in\mathbb{R}$ put
\[
(t)_{m}=t(t-1)\cdots(t-m+1)\qquad\bigl((t)_{0}=1\bigr).
\]
For every $k$ define the $n\times n$ matrix
\[
B^{(k)}
=\bigl[(b_{k,j})_{s}\bigr]_{0\le s\le n-1,\;1\le j\le n},
\]
a factorial Vandermonde; hence $\det B^{(k)}\neq 0$.

Set  
\[
u^{(k)}=\begin{pmatrix}-1\\0\\\vdots\\0\end{pmatrix},
\qquad
w^{(k)}=\begin{pmatrix}(b_{k,1})_{n}\\ \vdots\\ (b_{k,n})_{n}\end{pmatrix},
\]
and form the Kronecker products  
\[
B:=B^{(r)}\otimes\cdots\otimes B^{(1)},\qquad
u:=u^{(r)}\otimes\cdots\otimes u^{(1)}.
\]
All three objects have length $n^{\,r}$; $B$ is invertible.



2.  A linear system for the coefficients $a_{\,\dots}$.  
Apply $\partial^{\boldsymbol{\alpha}}$ to $(\star)$ and set
$x=\mathbf{1}$.

*  If $0\le\alpha_{k}\le n-1$ for every $k$ and
$\boldsymbol{\alpha}\neq\mathbf{0}$, the factor
$(1-x_{j})$ survives for some $j$, so the left-hand side vanishes at
$x=\mathbf{1}$.  Thus
\[
\sum_{i_{1},\dots ,i_{r}}
a_{i_{1},\dots ,i_{r}}
\prod_{k=1}^{r}(b_{k,i_{k}})_{\alpha_{k}}
=0.
\tag{1}
\]

*  For $\boldsymbol{\alpha}=\mathbf{0}$ we obtain
\[
1+\sum_{i_{1},\dots ,i_{r}}a_{i_{1},\dots ,i_{r}}=0,
\qquad\Longrightarrow\qquad
\sum_{i_{1},\dots ,i_{r}}a_{i_{1},\dots ,i_{r}}=-1.
\tag{2}
\]

Arrange the $n^{\,r}$ unknowns
$A=\operatorname{vec}[a_{i_{1},\dots ,i_{r}}]$.  
Conditions (1)-(2) are equivalent to the linear system
\[
B\,A = e,
\tag{3}
\]
where the right-hand side vector $e\in\mathbb{R}^{n^{\,r}}$ is defined by  

- \emph{Row-wise description}: $e$ has entry $-1$ in the row indexed by
$\boldsymbol{\alpha}=\mathbf{0}$ and $0$ in every other row.  

- \emph{Kronecker description}:  
\[
e=(-1)^{\,1-r}\,u,
\]
so that the very first component of $e$ equals $-1$ (not $(-1)^{\,r}$).

Because $B$ is nonsingular, (3) has the unique solution
\[
A=B^{-1}e.
\tag{4}
\]



3.  A high derivative links $f(\mathbf{1})$ to the $a$'s.  
Let $\boldsymbol{\beta}=(n,n,\dots ,n)$.  Differentiating $(\star)$ by
$\partial^{\boldsymbol{\beta}}$ and then putting $x=\mathbf{1}$ gives
\[
(-1)^{\,nr}\,(n!)^{\,r}\,f(\mathbf{1})
=\sum_{i_{1},\dots ,i_{r}}
a_{i_{1},\dots ,i_{r}}
\prod_{k=1}^{r}(b_{k,i_{k}})_{n}
=w^{\mathsf T}B^{-1}e,
\tag{5}
\]
where $w:=w^{(r)}\otimes\cdots\otimes w^{(1)}$.



4.  Kronecker factorisation.  
The scalar $w^{\mathsf T}B^{-1}e$ separates:
\[
w^{\mathsf T}B^{-1}e
=(-1)^{\,1-r}\,
\prod_{k=1}^{r}\bigl[(w^{(k)})^{\mathsf T}(B^{(k)})^{-1}u^{(k)}\bigr].
\tag{6}
\]
Thus we need the one-variable constant
\[
S_{k}:=(w^{(k)})^{\mathsf T}(B^{(k)})^{-1}u^{(k)}.
\]



5.  Evaluation of $S_{k}$.  
Fix $k$ and abbreviate $B=B^{(k)},\,w=w^{(k)},\,u=u^{(k)}$.
Cramer's rule gives
\[
B^{-1}u
=-\,\frac{\operatorname{adj}B_{\,\cdot ,1}}{\det B},
\qquad\Longrightarrow\qquad
S_{k}
=-\,\frac{w^{\mathsf T}\!\operatorname{adj}B_{\,\cdot ,1}}{\det B}.
\]
The numerator is
$\det B[1\leftarrow w]$, the determinant of the matrix obtained by
replacing the first \emph{row} of $B$ by $w^{\mathsf T}$.  A standard
factorial-Vandermonde computation yields
\[
\det B[1\leftarrow w]
=(-1)^{\,n+1}\Bigl(\prod_{j=1}^{n}b_{k,j}\Bigr)\det B,
\]
hence
\[
S_{k}=(-1)^{\,n}\prod_{j=1}^{n}b_{k,j}.
\tag{7}
\]



6.  Completion.  
Insert (7) into (6):
\[
w^{\mathsf T}B^{-1}e
=(-1)^{\,1-r}\,
\prod_{k=1}^{r}(-1)^{\,n}\prod_{j=1}^{n}b_{k,j}
=(-1)^{\,nr+1-r}\,
\prod_{k=1}^{r}\prod_{j=1}^{n}b_{k,j}.
\tag{8}
\]

Combine (5) with (8).  The factor $(-1)^{\,nr}$ cancels, leaving
\[
f(1,1,\dots ,1)
\;=\;
(-1)^{\,r-1}\;
\frac{\displaystyle\prod_{k=1}^{r}\prod_{j=1}^{n}b_{k,j}}
      {(n!)^{\,r}}.
\qquad\qquad\blacksquare
\]

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1. Higher dimensionality.  
   • The problem moves from one variable to r variables (r≥2), so the
     unknown polynomial lives in a space of significantly higher
     dimension.  
   • The number of unknown coefficients rises from n to n^{r}.  
   • Mixed partial derivatives and Kronecker-product linear algebra are
     required.

2. Additional constraints.  
   • All mixed derivatives of order < n in every direction must vanish at
     (1,…,1), producing a system of n^{r} simultaneous equations rather
     than n.

3. More sophisticated structure.  
   • The solution uses factorial Vandermonde matrices in each variable
     and their Kronecker product; this demands familiarity with tensor
     products, multi-index calculus, and properties of block determinants.

4. Deeper theory and multiple interacting concepts.  
   • One must combine multivariate calculus, combinatorial identities for
     falling factorials, determinant factorisation, and Kronecker
     geometry to isolate the single surviving mixed derivative and relate
     it to det M.  
   • The determinant evaluation generalises the classical Vandermonde
     argument to multi-linear algebra, a non-trivial extension.

Hence the enhanced variant is substantially more intricate than the
original (one variable, n equations, ordinary Vandermonde) and the
previous kernel variant (constant term 2, primes only).  Solving it
demands advanced multivariate techniques beyond routine pattern
matching.
